title: Computer Networks
description: From cables to the cloud — how packets actually travel.

lectures.title: Lecture 01 · Foundations
lectures.description: Layers, addresses and the shape of the internet.

cards.question: What problem does the layered network model solve?
cards.answer: It splits one impossible job into small, swappable pieces. Each layer only talks to the ones above and below it, so you can change Wi-Fi to Ethernet without rewriting the browser.

cards.question: What do the four TCP/IP layers roughly do?
cards.answer: Link moves bits over one hop, Internet routes packets across networks, Transport gives apps a reliable stream, Application is the actual protocol (HTTP, DNS…).

cards.question: What is the difference between an IP address and a MAC address?
cards.answer: A MAC address names a device on one local link; an IP address names a device on the whole internet and can change as it moves networks.

cards.question: What does DNS do?
cards.answer: It's the internet's phone book: it translates human names like example.com into the IP addresses machines actually route to.

cards.question: Why send data as packets instead of one continuous stream?
cards.answer: Packets can be routed independently, retried when lost, and interleaved with others — so one big transfer can't hog a link and failures stay local.

lectures.title: Lecture 02 · Transport
lectures.description: TCP, UDP and the promises they make.

cards.question: What guarantees does TCP add on top of IP?
cards.answer: Reliable, in-order delivery: it numbers bytes, re-sends what's lost, and reassembles everything in the right order for the app.

cards.question: What is the TCP three-way handshake?
cards.answer: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Both sides agree on starting sequence numbers before any data flows, so each knows the other is ready and reachable.

cards.question: When is UDP the better choice than TCP?
cards.answer: When speed beats perfection — live video, games, DNS. Losing a packet is fine; waiting to re-send it would hurt more than dropping it.

cards.question: What is congestion control, in one line?
cards.answer: Each sender slows down when the network looks overloaded (packets dropping) and speeds back up when it recovers — so no single flow drowns the link.
